title: Oklahoma State University — Watershed Hydrology & Rangeland Ecology (2 MS positions)
org: Stillwater, OK · Environmental Science Graduate Program
deadline: May 15, 2026 deadline passed — next cycle ~May 2027
body: Two fully funded MS assistantships — Neosho River Watershed, Oklahoma. Tar Creek Superfund Site contamination context. Position 1: Ecology/Rangeland — wildlife habitat, species of conservation need. Position 2: Hydrology/GIS — site suitability modeling, flood mitigation. Full stipend + tuition waiver + health insurance. Advisors: Dr. Scott Stoodley & Dr. Jaime Schussler. Email: esgp@okstate.edu
desc: Two fully funded MS assistantships for an interdisciplinary watershed conservation project in the Neosho River Watershed of northeastern Oklahoma — which includes the Tar Creek Superfund Site, one of the most historically significant mining-contaminated landscapes in the US. Position 1 (Ecology/Rangeland): wildlife habitat assessment, species of conservation need, restoration planning. Position 2 (Hydrology/GIS): watershed hydrology, site suitability modeling, flood mitigation, conservation planning. The contaminated watershed + GIS + field ecology combination is a direct methodological parallel to Erin’s Pigeon River lead contamination capstone. Advisors: Dr. Scott Stoodley & Dr. Jaime Schussler.
metaLine: Start: May 27–June 5, 2026  ·  Funding: Full stipend + tuition waiver + health insurance

extbody: About SREL The Savannah River Ecology Laboratory has operated for over 70 years as a field station near Aiken, SC. more: Why this is Erin's #1 match This project studies the same Hurricane Helene event that Erin researched for her senior capstone — just from a forest ecology rather than water quality angle. The field site (Pisgah National Forest near Asheville) is adjacent to where Erin conducted her Pigeon River Basin research. Her WCU background, Appalachian field experience, wildlife coursework, and NRM 479 mammalogy skills all directly support this position. She can honestly say she has studied the same event from a complementary angle — that's a compelling narrative. extbody: Three-year funded MS research assistantship starting January 2026 in the Forest Hydrology and Soils Lab (Dr. Courtney Siegert). NSF-FIRE project investigating how back-to-back extreme weather events drive forest hydrology and flammability in the southeastern US. Fully funded: full tuition waiver + $22k stipend + health insurance + conference travel support.

links.label: 🔗 Dr. Courtney Siegert — Faculty page (MSU Forestry)
🔗 Siegert Hydrolab — Forest Hydrology & Soils (lab site)

more: Project details This is an NSF-FIRE funded project investigating how "whiplashes" (back-to-back extreme wet/dry events) in moisture patterns drive forest hydrology and flammability across southeastern landscapes. The student will work with an interdisciplinary team at MSU, NC State, and the USDA Forest Service. Work includes quantifying regional fuel loadings and conducting field-scale fuel moisture dynamic experiments. Applying to environmental science, hydrology, or forestry backgrounds explicitly welcome. If this specific position is filled, contact Dr. Siegert about future openings or related projects. detail.v: About the program

The USGS Recent Graduate Program is a formal federal hiring pathway. Participants complete a structured 1–2 year developmental experience, receive mentoring, and can be non-competitively converted to permanent federal positions at the end. This is a meaningful career accelerator for someone targeting a federal water resources or environmental science career.
How to find the right position

Search USAJobs for "USGS Recent Graduate" and filter by series: 1315 (Hydrology), 1350 (Geology), 0401 (Biology), 0408 (Ecology). Also search 1301 (Physical Science). Prioritize Water Science Center positions — SAWSC (NC/SC/GA), Southeast (FL/AL/MS), and Appalachians WSC are the strongest regional fits.
